input.background: Table 19.3 contains data on the acceptance of applications to graduate programs at the University of California Berkeley for 1973. Assuming men and women who applied for admission were equally well-qualified, one would expect an equal proportion of acceptance by gender.
input.question: Was there sex bias in graduate school admissions? Did it favor male or female applicants?

output.answer: There is a slight bias for female applicants.

gender.description: The gender of the applicants to graduate programs.

admission_status_combined.description: The admission status of applicants (Accepted or Rejected) for both majors combined.

male_combined.description: The number of male applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for both majors combined.

female_combined.description: The number of female applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for both majors combined.

admission_status_major_1.description: The admission status of applicants (Accepted or Rejected) for Major 1.

male_major_1.description: The number of male applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for Major 1.

female_major_1.description: The number of female applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for Major 1.

admission_status_major_2.description: The admission status of applicants (Accepted or Rejected) for Major 2.

male_major_2.description: The number of male applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for Major 2.

female_major_2.description: The number of female applicants who were accepted, rejected, and total for Major 2.

meta_info.caption: Sex bias in graduate school admissions?
